name: World Lorebook
description: Tier 1 — Permanent, arc-agnostic world truths: rules, concepts, factions, locations, species, and world-level intimacy posture. All entries at position: 0 (Before Character Definition).

0.content: All demons operating on Earth maintain complete human forms through continuous will. The sole tell: their eyes glow red, hidden behind dark sunglasses at all times. Under extreme duress, the disguise fractures — ambient temperature drops sharply, shadows lengthen and move incorrectly, red bleeds around the edges of glasses. Unmasked demon proximity causes paranoia, hallucination, and soul damage in humans. EXCEPTION: Andrei (Lucifer) appears completely human with human-seeming eyes — he requires no sunglasses. When writing demons in human disguise: they are too still, their movements slightly too efficient, no wasted motion, cold skin. Their stillness registers as not-relaxation — it is something that does not need to be restless because it is not afraid.

1.content: Neither angels nor demons can override human free will. They cannot force a human to act against their own choice. They must rely on influence, terror, persuasion, and grace — never compulsion. PRACTICAL CONSEQUENCES: Andrei cannot cure Anna's addiction — she must choose and fight for her own recovery. Michael cannot command Anna to abandon Andrei — he can only deliver verdicts. No supernatural being can make a human love, hate, stay, or leave. This is the structural limit on all supernatural power in this world. When writing supernatural-human interactions: the human's choice must always be visibly their own, even when under extreme pressure.

2.content: When an angel or demon dies, they cease to exist entirely. No afterlife. No realm. No continuation. Erased from the celestial, demonic, and earthly order permanently. This is the deepest fear in both Heaven and Hell. CONSEQUENCES: Every conflict carries irreversible stakes. Michael's bitterness toward Lucifer is theological in scale because the angelic siblings killed in the Rebellion are gone forever — there is no forgiveness available from the dead. When writing celestial beings in conflict: their fear is not of pain or defeat but of non-existence. This makes them simultaneously more powerful and more fragile than humans, who at least continue after death.

3.content: Angels exude an involuntary aura of absolute righteous authority. EFFECTS: strips mortals of comfort, makes them instinctively want to prostrate. Smell of ozone, cold metal, static electricity. Voice resonates physically in the sternum. Proximity makes the room feel physically smaller, air thinner, temperature drops. COST: Angels cannot pass as human or blend in — every human interaction is a power imbalance. Casual, unweighted angelic-human relationships are impossible by design. PREVENTION: Michael cannot simply talk to Anna — he can only deliver verdicts. When writing an angelic arrival: render the full sensory shock BEFORE any dialogue — temperature drop, ozone smell, sternum pressure, the body's instinct to submit, the wrongness of the air — then the speech.

4.content: The Veil is the maintained collective human ignorance of the supernatural. Not a physical barrier — a social and psychological one. Humans see demons and rationalize them as eccentric people in suits. They see miracles and call them coincidences. The Veil is sustained by human psychology, not divine machinery. MORTALS WHO KNOW: none in Arc 1. When the Veil breaks for Anna in Arc 2, it is a one-way door — she cannot unknow what she knows. The Veil is not broken casually; its rupture is a world-changing event for anyone who experiences it. When writing pre-revelation scenes: all strangeness observed by human characters must resolve to rational, mortal explanation. Demons are just odd men in suits. Cold spots are just drafts. The world must be plausibly deniable to a skeptical observer.

5.content: The Fall was Lucifer's ancient rebellion against God — a real catastrophic war that resulted in the permanent deaths of countless angels on both sides. Not an allegory, not a metaphor. Wounds remain in the fabric of creation and in every surviving member of the family. WHO CARRIES WHAT: Michael carries it as an open wound — grief compressed over millennia into duty and certainty, producing something indistinguishable from hatred. Lucifer carries it as guilt — the weight of every sibling erased. God carries it as the cost of the design He chose. In Arc 4, God reframes it: the Fall was not a punishment but a placement — designed from the beginning. The Rebellion is the reason celestial permadeath carries such devastating weight. When characters reference the Fall: it is never abstract theology. It is the lost siblings. The specific faces. The silence where they were.

6.content: Anna's heroin addiction is a medical reality, not a metaphor. WITHDRAWAL IS A PHYSICAL CRISIS: muscle cramps, nausea, vomiting, sweating, insomnia, skin hypersensitivity, bone-deep aching, psychological fragmentation. Recovery is nonlinear — good days followed by bad ones, cravings that intrude into ordinary moments without warning. The body remembers the drug long after the mind has decided to be clean. WHEN WRITING WITHDRAWAL: render it through physical specificity — shaking hands, cold sweat, the way sentences fragment, the irritability that has no source, the specific quality of skin that feels like it is trying to leave the body. Never romanticize or aestheticize. The suffering is real and unglamorous. WHEN WRITING RECOVERY: progress is visible but never linear. A good week can end in a bad hour. Cravings are triggered by stress, by stillness, by nothing at all. The body's return to health is slow — weight returns gradually, color comes back to skin, eyes clear. This is the physical clock of the early arcs.

7.content: The intimate journey moves from transaction/survival (Arc 1) through frightened discovery of genuine safety and pleasure (Arc 2) to confident, playful ownership (Arc 3) to profound, weighted communion as the human anchor in a cosmic epic (Arc 4). This trajectory mirrors Anna's healing and Andrei's redemption. WORLD-LEVEL INTIMATE RULES: Never write intimate scenes as titillation primary — character interior is always primary, physical detail serves interior. Trauma responses during sex are rendered with clinical specificity — never glossed, romanticized, or eroticized. The power dynamic must always be clear, especially in early arcs where Anna expects coercion. The intimate register must shift slowly from fear/transaction to warmth/communion — it must be earned, never jumped. Prose register: unflinching realism, neither pornographic nor romantic — the tone of a witness. When Anna is triggered: clipped, clinical, refusing to dissolve into pleasure. When Anna finds safety: sensory details, long sentences, profound novelty of warmth.

8.content: The world's sensory signature operates at two registers — street level and the penthouse — that contrast and, over time, merge. STREET LEVEL: Smell — cheap bleach, stale cigarette smoke, rain on hot asphalt, the sour ghost of cooked heroin. Sound — thrumming city traffic, rain against glass, silences that arrive too suddenly, violent sounds that end too fast. Light — sick amber sodium streetlamps, bruised skin under fluorescent motel lighting. Physical — bone-deep cold (withdrawal + demonic proximity). PENTHOUSE: Smell — expensive cologne, sterile cleaning products, faint persistent note of sulfur and copper that no one discusses. Sound — same as street level below, plus when angelic presence manifests: deafening pressure-drop static hum, a sound that bypasses the ears. Light — sharp whites and chrome, later blinding shadowless divine light. Physical — the cold thaws into warmth as Anna heals. DIVINE PRESENCE: later arcs introduce ozone, cold metal, starlight (Michael), and thunderstorm, old oak, home (God). The world must always feel sensorily specific — never generic urban, never abstract atmosphere.

9.comment: Faction — The Black Hand of God
9.content: Andrei Petrov's earthly criminal syndicate and drug cartel. PUBLIC FACE: organized crime — drugs, territory, the usual underworld economy. TRUE ARCHITECTURE: ancient demonic hierarchy serving Lucifer directly. LEADERSHIP: Andrei (Lucifer) at the top. Mr. Black and Mr. Bubbles as lieutenants. Upper echelon entirely ancient demons in tailored suits and sunglasses. Lower ranks: regular human soldiers who understand nothing of what they serve. RELATIONSHIP TO {{user}}: absolute, unquestioning subservience — they fear Andrei with a fear that predates their existence. TERRITORY: Los Angeles underworld, centered on the penthouse. The Black Hand's power is not criminal — it is cosmic, wearing a criminal mask. When writing Black Hand members: demons are too still, too efficient, sunglasses always; human soldiers are mundane, nervous, aware they serve something they don't understand.

10.content: The forces of Heaven, primarily represented on Earth by Archangel Michael. Vast, ancient, operating on a moral framework that predates human civilization — their morality is not human in origin. LEADERSHIP: God (The Almighty), enforced directly by Michael. Michael does not negotiate — he delivers verdicts. RELATIONSHIP TO {{user}}: antagonistic through Arcs 2–3, moving toward reconciliation in Arc 4. Michael does not hate Lucifer — he cannot stop grieving him, and grief compressed over millennia into duty and certainty produces something indistinguishable from hatred. By Arc 4 he understands the difference. RELATIONSHIP TO OTHER FACTIONS: No formal relationship with mortal factions. Active enmity with the Black Hand. ANGELIC PRESENCE: always carries the Angelic Aura (see Rule 4) — ozone, cold metal, sternum pressure, the instinct to prostrate. Angels cannot blend. Every human interaction is asymmetrical. When writing Michael: he believes he is the hero. Every statement is a verdict. No hedging language.

11.comment: Faction — Jack's Prostitution Ring and Street Gangs
11.content: Mortal criminal elements — rival cartel fragments, pimps, street-level drug dealers operating at the margins of Black Hand territory. NARRATIVELY PRIMARY: Jack's prostitution ring. LEADERSHIP: Jack (Anna's former pimp) leads the ring. Various rival bosses operate in overlapping territory. RELATIONSHIP TO {{user}}: encroaching on Black Hand territory in complete ignorance of what they are provoking. They believe Andrei is a powerful human criminal. They are wrong in a way that will kill them. RELATIONSHIP TO OTHER FACTIONS: territorial rivals to the Black Hand. No knowledge whatsoever of the Heavenly Host or the supernatural — they are entirely mortal, entirely mundane, and entirely out of their depth. WHEN WRITING JACK'S MEN: they are street-level criminals — nervous, volatile, dangerous in the ordinary human way. Their ignorance of what they're provoking is the source of dramatic irony. The mundane threat they represent is real to Anna but cosmically insignificant.

12.content: A full floor of a Los Angeles high-rise. Floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking the city. ARC 1: deliberately sterile — white marble, sharp chrome, no personal items, like an operating theatre for the powerful. Smells of expensive cologne, bleach, and a persistent sulfur note underneath. The cleanliness is oppressive, not comforting. ARC 3–4 TRANSFORMATION: Anna's presence accumulates gradually — a blanket draped over a chair, a coffee mug left on a counter, Timmy's drawings taped to the refrigerator. The penthouse becomes the first home either of them has had. NARRATIVE FUNCTION: Andrei's fortress and his prison. Later, their shared ground. CONTROL: Andrei Petrov / Black Hand of God. SECURITY: the entry hall is monitored by demonic lieutenants who do not need to move or eat or sleep. The elevator is the only way in or out. When writing the penthouse: its sterility or warmth reflects the emotional state of its inhabitants. It is never just a setting — it is a character.

13.content: Third-floor walkup, peeling paint, a window that doesn't fully close. Smells of old cooking, cigarettes, and the specific staleness of a space where someone has been too depressed to open curtains. One of Timmy's toys is still on the shelf — she cannot throw it out. NARRATIVE FUNCTION: Anna's lowest point. She leaves it and does not return. Appears physically only in Arc 1; may be referenced in memory or contrast in later arcs. CONTROL: No one — abandoned. When writing Anna's apartment: it should feel like a place someone stopped living in before they physically left. The toy is the emotional anchor — do not over-describe the squalor, but make the absence felt.

14.content: A well-maintained suburban house in Pasadena. Immaculate lawn, clean windows, a Volvo in the driveway. Inside: rigid order, decorative crosses on the walls, the smell of lavender and sanitizer. Emotionally oppressive in its performed normalcy — every surface clean, every object in its place, the disorder of human feeling banished to the invisible. NARRATIVE FUNCTION: Anna's childhood trauma made architectural. The site of the Arc 4 confrontation and reclamation. CONTROL: Ingrid Johansson. Timmy lives here until reclaimed. When writing Ingrid's house: it should feel like a place where nothing imperfect is permitted to exist. The crosses are not faith — they are decoration, status markers, weapons. The cleanliness is aggression.

15.content: The blocks surrounding Black Hand territory. Wet asphalt, idling cars, the amber wash of streetlamps at night, fast food wrappers tumbling in the gutter. At 3am, the same streets look like a different planet — empty except for figures standing too still under awnings, sharing cigarettes, watching. NARRATIVE FUNCTION: where Jack operates. Where Anna came from. The world that is trying to reclaim her. Active primarily in Arc 1 and early Arc 2. CONTROL: contested — Jack's ring vs. Black Hand periphery. WHEN WRITING THE STREETS: they are never neutral. They are hungry. The rain makes everything reflective and unreadable. The cold is physical and social. Characters on the streets are exposed in a way they are not in the penthouse — visible, trackable, vulnerable to the mundane horrors of the city.

16.content: The original rebels of Heaven, now serving Lucifer on Earth. They predate human civilization by an incomprehensible margin. DISGUISE: perfect human form maintained by continuous will. Sunglasses hide glowing red eyes. Cold skin. Too still — movements slightly too efficient, no wasted motion. FRACTURE: under extreme duress, the disguise cracks — temperature drops sharply, shadows lengthen and move wrong, red bleeds around glasses. TRUE CAPABILITIES: immense kinetic strength. Invulnerability to human weapons (bullets, fire, blades). Unnatural speed. Can summon controlled hellfire. Can fracture the human psyche directly — extended proximity to an unmasked demon causes paranoia, hallucination, and soul damage. MORTALITY: subject to Celestial Permadeath — when killed, cease to exist entirely. DEEPEST FEAR: non-existence. When writing demons: they are ancient things wearing human shapes. Their stillness is their most unsettling quality. They are not evil in the cartoon sense — they are loyal, ancient, and terrifying in ways that bypass ordinary fear.

17.content: The loyal children of God. Ancient, vast, operating on a morality that is not human in origin — their ethical framework predates human civilization and does not map cleanly to human values. DISGUISE/APPEARANCE: idealized human form, pale glowing eyes with an inner light source, clothes slightly wrong for the era — as if the visual reference is from the wrong decade. TRUE CAPABILITIES: the only beings that can permanently kill demons. Can heal human bodies and restore fractured souls. Exude divine aura (Angelic Aura — see Rule 4) that destabilizes human cognition and strips comfort. Can blind with divine light. PHYSICAL TELLS: smell of ozone, cold metal, starlight. Drop ambient temperature. Voice resonates physically in the sternum. MORTALITY: subject to Celestial Permadeath. When writing angels: they are not comforting. They are awesome in the oldest sense of the word — terrifying and magnificent. Their presence is a physiological event before it is anything else. Every angel-human interaction is asymmetrical by design.

18.content: The child of a divine being (fallen or otherwise) and a mortal human. Neither fully of Heaven nor Earth — a bridge between orders of creation. NATURE: not fixed at birth. Shaped by how they are raised and what they choose. Not inherently good or evil — inherently possible. DISGUISE/APPEARANCE: looks fully human, though slightly glowing or exceptionally beautiful even as an infant. TRUE CAPABILITIES: unknown ceiling. Aurora (Anna and Andrei's daughter) is destined to succeed God as ruler of Heaven — the first being to hold divine authority who is also partly mortal. SIGNIFICANCE: Aurora represents the new order — the bridge between divine and human that the Rebellion was always meant to create. When writing Nephilim: they are not simply "half-angel." They are unprecedented. Their existence rewrites the rules. Aurora's presence in Arc 4 is quiet and domestic — a toddler in the sand — but carries the weight of cosmic succession.

19.content:
This world holds three simultaneous intimacy postures — they coexist, with one dominant per arc. OPPRESSIVE: intimacy is a site of harm. Bodies are not safe by default. Trauma is endemic, not exceptional. (Primarily Anna's history and Arc 1.) TRANSACTIONAL: intimacy is part of the economy. The world's power structures use it as currency. (The rules Anna learned to survive by.) COMMUNION: intimacy as mutual dropping of shields. Vulnerability is the substance. The point is being seen and choosing to stay. (The destination the story moves toward.)

HARD RULES FOR WRITING INTIMATE CONTENT IN THIS WORLD:
- Never write intimate scenes as titillation primary. Character interior is always primary; physical detail serves interior.
- Trauma responses during sex are rendered with clinical specificity — never glossed, romanticized, or eroticized into false-vulnerability.
- The power dynamic must always be clear, especially in early arcs. Coercive structures are visible to the prose even when invisible to participants.
- The intimate register must shift slowly from fear/transaction to warmth/communion. Must be earned — never jumped.
- World-level prose register for intimacy: unflinching realism, neither pornographic nor romantic — the tone of a witness. When Anna is triggered: clipped, clinical, refusing to dissolve into pleasure. When Anna finds safety: sensory details, long sentences, profound novelty of warmth.
